learning_objectives: Apply realist, liberal, and constructivist theories to explain why IOs exist
Distinguish between functionalism, neofunctionalism, and regime theory
Evaluate the concept of international regimes
readings: Karns, Mingst & Stiles, International Organizations Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Why do sovereign states create international organizations that constrain their freedom? Different theoretical perspectives offer competing explanations.
key_concepts: Functionalism: technical cooperation spills over into political integration (Mitrany)
Neofunctionalism: supranational institutions drive further integration (Haas)
Regime theory: principles, norms, rules, and decision-making procedures
Principal-agent theory: states (principals) delegate to IOs (agents)
IO autonomy: when agents exceed their mandates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Applying regime theory to the international climate regime from UNFCCC to Paris Agreement

review.value: Krasner's definition: implicit or explicit principles, norms, rules, and decision-making procedures around which actors' expectations converge in a given issue area
The theory that integration in one sector creates pressures for integration in adjacent sectors (spillover), driven by supranational institutions and transnational interest groups

learning_objectives: Map the UN system: General Assembly, Security Council, ECOSOC, Secretariat, ICJ
Analyze the veto power and Security Council reform debates
Evaluate UN effectiveness in peace and security

lecture_notes.introduction: The United Nations is the most ambitious international organization ever created. Understanding its structure and limitations is essential for anyone in international studies.
key_concepts: General Assembly: universal membership, one-state-one-vote
Security Council: P5 veto, enforcement powers under Chapter VII
Secretariat and the role of the Secretary-General
UN specialized agencies: WHO, UNESCO, UNHCR, WFP
Security Council reform: demands for expansion and veto reform
lecture_notes.demonstration: Security Council simulation: voting on a resolution authorizing peacekeeping with veto dynamics
setup_requirements: Security Council role assignments (P5 + elected members)

review.value: The power of the five permanent Security Council members (US, UK, France, Russia, China) to block any non-procedural resolution, the most consequential feature of the UN system
The UN Charter chapter authorizing the Security Council to determine threats to peace and take binding enforcement measures including sanctions and military force

learning_objectives: Compare the institutional designs of major regional organizations
Analyze the EU as the most integrated regional organization
Evaluate the effectiveness of regional organizations in their respective contexts
readings: Acharya, Constructing a Security Community in Southeast Asia Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Regional organizations vary enormously in their design, authority, and effectiveness. Comparing them reveals how context shapes institutional choices.
key_concepts: The EU: supranational institutions, single market, common currency, democratic deficit
The African Union: from OAU to AU, peace and security architecture, Agenda 2063
ASEAN: the ASEAN Way, consensus, non-interference, economic integration
NATO: collective defense, burden-sharing, expansion controversies
Design choices: intergovernmental vs. supranational, consensus vs. majority
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative table: institutional design features of EU, AU, ASEAN, and NATO

review.value: Supranational (EU model): institutions have authority over member states. Intergovernmental (ASEAN model): decisions require state consent and respect sovereignty.
ASEAN's diplomatic norm emphasizing consultation, consensus, non-interference in internal affairs, and quiet diplomacy over confrontation

class_schedule.topic: International Economic Organizations: IMF, World Bank, WTO
learning_objectives: Analyze the governance structures and decision-making of IEOs
Evaluate the IMF and World Bank's evolving roles in development
Assess WTO dispute settlement and the crisis of multilateral trade governance
readings: Stiglitz, Globalization and Its Discontents Ch. 2 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: International economic organizations wield enormous influence over the global economy. Their governance raises critical questions about power, representation, and accountability.
key_concepts: IMF: surveillance, lending, conditionality, quota system and weighted voting
World Bank: development lending, knowledge bank, safeguard policies
WTO: trade liberalization, dispute settlement, Doha Round stalemate
Governance reform: voice and representation for developing countries
Alternative institutions: AIIB, NDB, regional development banks
lecture_notes.demonstration: IMF quota simulation: calculating voting power and analyzing power distribution

review.value: Policy conditions attached to IMF loans requiring borrowers to implement structural reforms, criticized for imposing austerity on vulnerable populations
The quasi-judicial mechanism for resolving trade disputes between WTO members, considered the most effective enforcement mechanism in international law

class_schedule.topic: NGOs, Transnational Advocacy, and Global Civil Society
learning_objectives: Analyze the role of NGOs in global governance
Apply the boomerang model and transnational advocacy network theory
Evaluate the legitimacy and accountability of non-state actors in global governance
readings: Keck & Sikkink, Activists Beyond Borders Ch. 1-2 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Non-governmental organizations have become powerful actors in global governance, but their growing influence raises important questions about legitimacy and accountability.
key_concepts: Types of NGOs: advocacy, operational, hybrid
Transnational advocacy networks: boomerang and spiral models
NGO functions: information, advocacy, monitoring, service delivery
NGO-IO relationships: consultation, partnership, co-optation
Legitimacy and accountability challenges for NGOs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study: the International Campaign to Ban Landmines as a transnational advocacy network
setup_requirements: Landmine campaign timeline and network map

review.value: When domestic groups cannot influence their government, they reach out to international allies (NGOs, IOs, foreign governments) who pressure the target state from outside
Networks of activists, NGOs, and international organizations linked by shared values that exchange information and use strategic framing to promote normative change

class_schedule.topic: The Future of International Organizations: Reform and Adaptation
learning_objectives: Assess major proposals for UN and IO reform
Analyze the challenge of institutional adaptation to new global problems
Evaluate the tension between legitimacy and effectiveness in global governance

lecture_notes.introduction: International organizations created for the post-WWII world face 21st-century challenges. Can they adapt, or are new institutional forms needed?
key_concepts: UN reform: Security Council expansion, budgetary reform, coherence
Institutional gridlock: growing multipolarity, harder issues, institutional inertia
Minilateralism: small-group alternatives (G7, G20, coalitions of the willing)
Networked governance: public-private partnerships, multi-stakeholder platforms
Legitimacy-effectiveness trade-off in institutional design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Institutional design exercise: designing a new international organization for AI governance
setup_requirements: IO design template (mandate, membership, decision rules, funding, enforcement)

review.value: Hale et al.'s argument that the very success of postwar institutions created conditions (multipolarity, harder problems, institutional complexity) that now prevent further cooperation
The strategy of working with the smallest number of countries necessary to achieve the largest impact, bypassing the unanimity requirements of universal organizations

quizzes.topic: IO Theory and the UN System

questions.question: Explain regime theory as it applies to international organizations. What are the four components of an international regime according to Krasner, and provide an example of an existing regime.
questions.answer: Krasner defines international regimes as sets of implicit or explicit (1) principles -- beliefs of fact, causation, and rectitude, (2) norms -- standards of behavior, (3) rules -- specific prescriptions and proscriptions, and (4) decision-making procedures -- prevailing practices for making and implementing collective choice. For example, the international trade regime includes the principle of comparative advantage, the norm of non-discrimination (MFN), the rules of WTO agreements, and the dispute settlement procedures of the WTO.

questions.question: Why is the P5 veto in the UN Security Council considered both essential and problematic? What is the strongest argument for and against reforming the veto?
questions.answer: The veto is essential because it ensures that enforcement actions have the backing of the major military powers, preventing the UN from mandating actions it cannot implement and keeping great powers engaged in the system. Without the veto, great powers would likely leave or ignore the UN (as happened with the League of Nations). It is problematic because it allows any P5 member to block action regardless of the merits, leading to paralysis on issues where P5 interests diverge (e.g., Syria, Israel-Palestine). The strongest argument for reform is democratic legitimacy: the P5 reflects 1945 power realities, not the 21st-century world. The strongest argument against is practical: reform requires P5 consent, and no P5 member will voluntarily surrender its veto.

rubric.key: Explains why the veto is essential
Presents balanced arguments for and against reform

questions.question: Compare the EU's supranational approach to integration with ASEAN's intergovernmental approach. What explains the different institutional choices?
questions.answer: The EU features supranational institutions (European Commission, European Parliament, European Court of Justice) with authority over member states, majority voting in many policy areas, and directly binding law. ASEAN operates on consensus, non-interference, and voluntary compliance with minimal supranational authority. The differences are explained by context: Europe's experience of devastating wars created willingness to pool sovereignty for peace; relatively homogeneous political systems (democracies) facilitated deep integration. Southeast Asia's colonial legacies made sovereignty sacrosanct; diverse political systems (democracies and authoritarian regimes) make pooling sovereignty politically impossible. ASEAN's 'soft institutionalism' reflects its members' prioritization of sovereignty over efficiency.

rubric.key: Explains the contextual factors behind the differences

questions.question: Explain the boomerang model of transnational advocacy. Under what conditions is it most likely to succeed?
questions.answer: The boomerang model (Keck and Sikkink) describes how domestic groups that are blocked from influencing their own government 'boomerang' outward to international allies (foreign NGOs, IOs, sympathetic governments), who then pressure the target state from above and outside. It is most likely to succeed when: (1) the issue involves bodily harm to vulnerable individuals (high moral leverage), (2) there is a clear causal chain between policy and harm, (3) the target state is vulnerable to international pressure (economically dependent, reputation-sensitive), and (4) strong transnational networks exist to coordinate pressure and amplify the message.

major_assignments.title: International Organization Effectiveness Assessment
major_assignments.description: Select an international organization and conduct a comprehensive assessment of its effectiveness, governance, and reform needs. Apply IO theory to evaluate its design and performance.
objectives: Analyze IO institutional design through theoretical lenses
Evaluate organizational effectiveness using specific criteria

criteria: Clear application of IO theory to the case
Appropriate use of regime theory, principal-agent, or functionalism
Engagement with academic literature on the IO
Consideration of both successes and failures
Proposals grounded in analysis of current weaknesses
Awareness of political constraints on reform
Effective use of data and visuals
Theory-driven analysis of international organization effectiveness
Evidence-based assessment of governance and performance

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on IO theory, structures, and current events

simulation_participation.description: Engagement in Security Council and IO simulations

major_assignments.description: IO effectiveness assessment paper and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering IO theory, the UN system, and regional organizations

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam on global governance institutions and reform

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance required. Simulation absences cannot be made up and result in zero for that exercise.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers penalized 5% per day. No extensions within 48 hours of deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Simulation preparation is collaborative. All written analysis must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism results in course failure. Proper citation of all IO documents and academic sources required.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to UN document databases, IO websites, and academic journals through university library.
